title: Religion
overview: Religion has played a major role in shaping life, culture, and values in the United Kingdom. Freedom of religion is protected by law, allowing people to follow any faith or none. Although the country's history is closely linked with Christianity, today Britain is home to many different religions and beliefs. This diversity is an important part of modern British identity.

study_sections.heading: Christianity and the Church
study_sections.content: Church of England is the established church in England. The Monarch is Supreme Governor of Church of England. Archbishop of Canterbury is the spiritual leader. Church of Scotland is Presbyterian and independent of state. About half of UK population identify as Christian.

study_sections.heading: Religious Freedom
study_sections.content: Human Rights Act 1998 protects freedom of religion. Equality Act 2010 promotes equal treatment of all faiths. People can follow any faith or none. Islam is the second largest religion in UK.

study_sections.heading: Major Festivals
study_sections.content: Christian: Christmas (25 Dec), Easter (spring). Islamic: Eid. Hindu: Diwali (Festival of Lights). Jewish: Hanukkah (miracle of oil lamps). Sikh: Vaisakhi (founding of Khalsa). Buddhist: Wesak/Vesak (Buddha's birth and enlightenment).

study_sections.heading: Places of Worship
study_sections.content: Muslims worship in mosques. Sikhs worship in gurdwaras. Jewish people worship in synagogues. Canterbury Cathedral is centre of Church of England. Monarch appoints Archbishop on PM's advice.

key_facts: The UK has a long history of religious belief, tolerance, and diversity with Christianity as the largest faith.
As a secular democracy, the UK protects freedom to practise any religion or none.
The Church of England is the established church in England, blending Protestant and Catholic traditions.
The monarch is Supreme Governor of the Church of England and appoints bishops on the Prime Minister’s advice.
The Archbishop of Canterbury leads the Church of England, joined by the Archbishop of York, and 26 bishops sit in the House of Lords as Lords Spiritual.
The Church of Scotland is Presbyterian and independent, while Wales and Northern Ireland have no established church.
Other Christian denominations include Roman Catholic, Baptist, Methodist, and Pentecostal communities.
Christian festivals such as Christmas and Easter shape public holidays.
Other major religions recognised in the UK include Islam, Hinduism, Sikhism, Judaism, and Buddhism, each with their own places of worship.
The 2021 Census showed increasing religious diversity and more people identifying with no religion.
Freedom of thought, conscience, and religion is protected by the Human Rights Act 1998.
Schools teach about different religions to promote understanding and respect.
Interfaith organisations encourage dialogue and cooperation between faith communities.
Religious buildings contribute to the UK’s cultural and architectural heritage.
Faith communities support moral values, social care, and charity work, helping diverse beliefs coexist peacefully.
memory_hook: Imagine UK faiths as a mosaic—cathedrals, mosques, temples, and secular spaces forming one picture held together by freedom and respect.
